events.title: Celebrate Juneteenth in Historic Seneca Village (2026)
events.summary: Celebrate the spirit of community and the history of Seneca Village with a special performance of music, dance, and spoken word.
events.description: Celebrate the spirit of community and the history of Seneca Village with a special performance of music, dance, and spoken word.

meta.og_title: Celebrate Juneteenth in Historic Seneca Village (2026)
meta.og_description: Celebrate the spirit of community and the history of Seneca Village with a special performance of music, dance, and spoken word.

schema_org.description: Celebrate the spirit of community and the history of Seneca Village with a special performance of music, dance, and spoken word.
schema_org.name: Celebrate Juneteenth in Historic Seneca Village (2026)

detail_page_data.location_detail: Seneca Village Landscape This Juneteenth, celebrate with the Central Park Conservancy, Broadway’s DeWitt Fleming, Jr., and an intergenerational community of performers in the historic Seneca Village landscape in Central Park. Interpretive music, dance, and spoken word will transport you to Seneca Village in 1845, where Black New Yorkers built their own homes, churches, and a school. The performance will explore how Andrew Williams’s 1825 land purchase evolved into a thriving community, which existed along what is now the Park’s perimeter from West 82nd to 89th Streets. We invite you to engage with the unique history of Seneca Village—deepening our collective awareness of this significant 19th-century free Black community while bridging past and present. Directed by Tony Award-winning Broadway legend Ben Harney, the performance was written and conceived by DeWitt Fleming, Jr. and co-produced by DeW It Right Productions, with original works by Markeisha Ensley, Mikumari Caiyhe, and Mercedes White. No registration required for this free community event. The performance starts at 1:00 pm , featuring: Broadway performer DeWitt Fleming, Jr. Broadway performer Jennie Harney Fleming Trumpeter, composer, and educator Alphonso Horne Poet and emcee Mikumari Caiyhe Writer, composer, and singer Markeisha Ensley Master percussionist Kahlil Kwame Bell Christian Cultural Center drama students Harlem School of the Arts drama and music students Bring a blanket to sit on and enjoy the performance in comfort! The Seneca Village landscape is between West 85th Street and West Drive. Enter the Park at W 85th St. This event is part of Seneca Village: Toward a Permanent Commemoration , an ongoing program series funded by the Mellon Foundation. This series will culminate in a community-informed framework guiding the future design of a permanent commemoration of Seneca Village in Central Park. Join the Seneca Village mailing list to receive event updates, Seneca Village history, and more! Email Address *
detail_page_data.description_detail: Celebrate the spirit of community and the history of Seneca Village with a special performance of music, dance, and spoken word.

events.title: Before, there was Seneca Village!
events.summary: Watch as Seneca Village is brought to life through an interpretive performance.
events.description: Watch as Seneca Village is brought to life through an interpretive performance.

meta.og_description: Watch as Seneca Village is brought to life through an interpretive performance.

schema_org.description: Watch as Seneca Village is brought to life through an interpretive performance.
schema_org.name: Before, there was Seneca Village!

detail_page_data.location_detail: Seneca Village Landscape Join us in the historic Seneca Village landscape in Central Park for a performance that takes you back to Seneca Village in 1845. Interpretive music, dance, and spoken word will bring to life a world where Black New Yorkers built their own homes, churches, and a school, exploring how Andrew Williams's and others’ 1825 land purchase evolved into a thriving community. Performances by: DeWitt Fleming, Jr., Broadway performer Alphonso Horne, musician Mikumari Caiyhe, spoken word artist Bring a blanket to sit on and enjoy the performance in comfort! The Seneca Village landscape is between West 85th Street and West Drive. Enter the Park at West 85th Street. This event is part of Seneca Village: Toward a Permanent Commemoration , an ongoing program series funded by the Mellon Foundation. This series will culminate in a community-informed framework guiding the future design of a permanent commemoration of Seneca Village in Central Park. Join the Seneca Village mailing list to receive event news, Seneca Village history, and more! Email Address *

events.title: Founders’ Day: 200th Anniversary of Seneca Village
events.summary: Join the Central Park Conservancy for a music-filled service at AME Zion Church in Harlem to honor the 200th anniversary of the first land purchase in Seneca Village.
events.description: Join the Central Park Conservancy for a music-filled service at AME Zion Church in Harlem to honor the 200th anniversary of the first land purchase in Seneca Village.

meta.og_title: Founders’ Day: 200th Anniversary of Seneca Village
meta.og_description: Join the Central Park Conservancy for a music-filled service at AME Zion Church in Harlem to honor the 200th anniversary of the first land purchase in…

schema_org.description: Join the Central Park Conservancy for a music-filled service at AME Zion Church in Harlem to honor the 200th anniversary of the first land purchase in Seneca Village.
schema_org.name: Founders’ Day: 200th Anniversary of Seneca Village

detail_page_data.location_detail: Mother AME Zion Church, 140 West 137th Street, New York, NY 10030 200 years ago, on September 17, 1825, congregant Andrew Williams and the trustees of the AME Zion Church were among the first to purchase land in the area between West 82nd and 89th Streets, which would become Seneca Village. On Sunday, September 14, join the Central Park Conservancy for a music-filled service at the historic AME Zion Church in Harlem—the first Black church in New York City—to honor this significant anniversary (in collaboration with the James L. Varick Community Center, Inc.). Through the sermon, live music, and a conversation with historians, discover the communal roots of this thriving, predominantly African-American 19th-century community. Service will begin at 11:00 AM, followed by a discussion between AME Zion Church leaders and a Seneca Village historian. Members of the Institute for the Exploration of Seneca Village History and the Envisioning Seneca Village team will also be present to answer questions about their research. Light refreshments will be available after service. Event Schedule Church Service: 11:00 AM Refreshments / Open House: 12:30 PM–1:00 PM Dabney N. Montgomery Heritage Center (AME Zion History Museum) Institute for the Exploration of Seneca Village History Envisioning Seneca Village Discussion: 1:00 PM This event is part of Seneca Village: Toward a Permanent Commemoration , an ongoing program series funded by the Mellon Foundation. This series will culminate in a community-informed framework guiding the future design of a permanent commemoration of Seneca Village in Central Park. Join the Seneca Village mailing list to receive event news, Seneca Village history, and more! Sign Up
detail_page_data.description_detail: Join the Central Park Conservancy for a music-filled service at AME Zion Church in Harlem to honor the 200th anniversary of the first land purchase in Seneca Village.

events.title: Central Park Farm Stand
events.summary: The Central Park Farm Stand offers fresh, seasonal produce grown with care by BIPOC-owned farms.
events.description: The Central Park Farm Stand offers fresh, seasonal produce grown with care by BIPOC-owned farms.

meta.og_description: The Central Park Farm Stand offers fresh, seasonal produce grown with care by BIPOC-owned farms.

schema_org.description: The Central Park Farm Stand offers fresh, seasonal produce grown with care by BIPOC-owned farms.
schema_org.name: Central Park Farm Stand

detail_page_data.location_detail: 110th Street & Malcolm X Boulevard Note: The farm stand has closed for the season; check back here or sign up for our email newsletter to get the latest on 2026 farm stand dates and other Conservancy updates. The Central Park Conservancy is delighted to partner with Corbin Hill Food Project to bring fresh, healthy, and affordable food options to our neighbors through the Central Park Farm Stand in summer and fall. The stand accepts SNAP, EBT, FMNP, and WIC benefits, ensuring access for all. The Central Park Farm Stand offers locally sourced produce from BIPOC-owned farms in the Hudson Valley, promoting food access, sustainable agriculture, healthy eating habits, and environmental conservation through community engagement. This community-focused offering is part of a vibrant local food ecosystem, creating economic opportunities for BIPOC farmers and small producers while bringing fresh, locally grown produce to the community. From Farm to Market: Hear from Corbin Hill Farmers Over the Years Ismail “We create markets for growers to sell in the very communities they come from. Bringing Black- and Brown-grown products into this space is truly a joy—and we’re proud to have the farmers here with us. It’s a one-of-a-kind experience you can’t find anywhere else in the City.” –Ismail Samad, Corbin Hill Food Project Gaby “Our main goal—our mission—is to feed the community we belong to. For us, it’s deeply important to bring fresh food back into the City. You get the food you deserve at the price you deserve—while supporting your local economy and local farmers.” –Gaby, Farmer at Yara Farm Chef Louise “We’re approaching food sovereignty, we’re addressing food insecurity, and we’re bringing all these great things to people in the neighborhood that can utilize it.” –Chef Louise, Corbin Hill Food Project Ismail “We create markets for growers to sell in the very communities they come from. Bringing Black- and Brown-grown products into this space is truly a joy—and we’re proud to have the farmers here with us. It’s a one-of-a-kind experience you can’t find anywhere else in the City.” –Ismail Samad, Corbin Hill Food Project Gaby “Our main goal—our mission—is to feed the community we belong to. For us, it’s deeply important to bring fresh food back into the City. You get the food you deserve at the price you deserve—while supporting your local economy and local farmers.” –Gaby, Farmer at Yara Farm Chef Louise “We’re approaching food sovereignty, we’re addressing food insecurity, and we’re bringing all these great things to people in the neighborhood that can utilize it.” –Chef Louise, Corbin Hill Food Project

events.title: Open House New York: The Blockhouse

schema_org.description: Event page for Open House New York at Chess & Checkers House in Central Park, NYC
schema_org.name: Open House New York: The Blockhouse

detail_page_data.location_detail: Blockhouse See inside Central Park’s oldest surviving structure! Located on a prominent rock outcrop in the North Woods, the Blockhouse was part of a fortification system built to defend New York from the British during the War of 1812. For one day only, join Open House New York and the Central Park Conservancy to explore the Blockhouse and learn about the history of the site with the Conservancy’s expert guides.
detail_page_data.description_detail: Event page for Open House New York at Chess & Checkers House in Central Park, NYC
